coredata.dc:title: Smart choice of carbon dots as a dual-mode onsite nanoplatform for the trace level detection of Cr2O7 2-
coredata.dc:creator: Qiao, Gongxi
Lu, Dong
Tang, Yiping
Gao, Jinwei
Wang, Qianming
coredata.dc:description: Efficient ways for the trace level monitoring of Cr2O7 2− have generated considerable attention in the fields of medical diagnosis and environmental protection. In current research, the water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) have been developed via the precursors of 1,3-phenylenediamineand citric acid. The quantum dots with particle size of 3–4 nm have been prepared based on a simple one-pot ultrasonic irradiation method and demonstrate bright blue emission at 440 nm. Its excitation-dependent feature has been explored and the achieved emission evolves from blue (440 nm) to green luminescence (528 nm). Photophysical studies support that CDs can be remarkably quenched by the presence of chromate due to inner filter effect. In addition, the absorption curves demonstrate that carbon dots possess highly selective and sensitive responses to Cr2O7 2− through color changes. The dual sensing modes (fluorescence and colorimetric detection) can be well-established for the recognition of chromate and the detection limits are calculated to be as low as 140 nM and 410 nM respectively. To further evaluate the analytical potentials of such nano-platform, the concentration of Cr2O7 2− have been determined in real water and river samples.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Ultrasonic
Sensor
Luminescence

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Analysis of Cr2O7 2− in real water samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: a) Schematic representation of carbon dots for Cr2O72- ions detection. b) Synthetic route by using citric acid and 1,3-phenylenediamine as precursors.

a) TEM image of the CDs. b) HRTEM image with lattice fringes. c) Fourier-transformed HRTEM image of the CDs. d) Electron diffraction of the CDs with indexed Bragg reflections. e) size distribution after dispersion in deionized water (according to DLS).
Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) spectrum of CDs.
a) XRD pattern of the CDs. b) FT-IR spectrum of CDs.

a) Emission and excitation spectra and UV–Vis absorbance of the CDs in deionized water, Inset show UV–Vis absorbance of the CDs from 320 nm to 400 nm b) Emission spectra of CDs, citric acid and m-phenylenediamine. c) Emission spectra of the CDs dispersed in pure DMF, DMF mixed with distilled water (1:1 ratio) and distilled water (DIW). d) Effects of pH on the emission intensity (F/F0).
a) Lifetime measurement of CDs. b) Emission spectra of the CDs upon excitation at different wavelengths.
Fluorescence spectra of the CDs with addition of Cr2O7 2− ions solution (0–5 μM).

Fluorescence intensity (F/F0) of the CDs toward various interfering substance in deionized water. (Cr2O7 2−, MnO4 −, NO2 −, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, (SCN)-, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, H2O2: 300 nM; Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Zn2+, Al3+: 500 nM).
Photographs of CDs solution after the addition of different species under the irradiation by a 365 nm UV lamp.
Emission and excitation spectra of the CDs in deionized water and UV–Vis absorbance of Cr2O7 2− in deionized water.
UV–Vis absorbance of the CDs sensor with the addition of 0–25 μM Cr2O7 2−.

Photographs of the CDs sensor with different interfering substances and Cr2O7 2− under sunlight. (Cr2O7 2−, MnO4 −, NO2 −, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−, SO4 2−, S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, (SCN)-, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, H2O2:300 nM; Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+, Al3+: 500 nM.
Detection of Cr2O7 2− in local Zhujiang river water.
Detection of Cr2O7 2− in drinking water.

head.ce:title: Smart choice of carbon dots as a dual-mode onsite nanoplatform for the trace level detection of Cr2O7 2-

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment, Ministry of Education, School of Chemistry & Environment, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Theoretical Chemistry of Environment

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Material Science and Engineering, Zhejiang University of Technology, Hangzhou, Zhejiang, 310014, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Material Science and Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Transparent Conductive Materials, South China Normal University, Guangzhou, 510006, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Guangdong Provincial Engineering Technology Research Center for Transparent Conductive Materials

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Efficient ways for the trace level monitoring of Cr2O7 2− have generated considerable attention in the fields of medical diagnosis and environmental protection. In current research, the water-soluble carbon dots (CDs) have been developed via the precursors of 1,3-phenylenediamineand citric acid. The quantum dots with particle size of 3–4 nm have been prepared based on a simple one-pot ultrasonic irradiation method and demonstrate bright blue emission at 440 nm. Its excitation-dependent feature has been explored and the achieved emission evolves from blue (440 nm) to green luminescence (528 nm). Photophysical studies support that CDs can be remarkably quenched by the presence of chromate due to inner filter effect. In addition, the absorption curves demonstrate that carbon dots possess highly selective and sensitive responses to Cr2O7 2− through color changes. The dual sensing modes (fluorescence and colorimetric detection) can be well-established for the recognition of chromate and the detection limits are calculated to be as low as 140 nM and 410 nM respectively. To further evaluate the analytical potentials of such nano-platform, the concentration of Cr2O7 2− have been determined in real water and river samples.

ce:section.ce:para: Low dimensional nanoscale materials are known as quantum dots and have aroused great interests in terms of their quantum size effects. However, the high toxicity, and hydrophobicity in CdSe, CdTe, and Ag2S will seriously restrict their application [1–3]. Hence, the search for new alternatives is a real challenge. In recently years, a variety of novel carbon-based nanomaterials including carbon nanotube, graphene, fullerene and nanohorns has been selected due to their unique features. Especially the discovery of carbon quantum dots (CDs) may gain advantages over the traditional quantum materials and possess the properties of low toxicity, biocompatibility, high chemical stability, and strong fluorescence [4–6]. Typical carbon dots preparation methods can be summarized into two strategies and these processes have been mentioned as top-down and bottom-up approaches. In top-down treatment, the raw material with large particle size has been decreased to expected dimensions via laser ablation, chemical or electrochemical oxidation. During bottom-up synthesis, the nanomaterials are well-formed from specific molecular precursors such as dopamine, glutathione and melamine based on a series of efficient ways including pyrolysis, microwave-assistance and hydrothermal [7]. In contrast, the ultrasonic vibrating will be considered as a simple, cost-effective and environmental-friendly way to obtain nano-sized particles efficiently. The literature reported the use of ascorbic acid and ammonia to fabricate nitrogen doped CDs with emissions by ultrasonicated synthesis [8]. Park et al. synthesized the desired material by the ultrasonic oscillation method. Carbon dots with high photostability, size uniformity, and low cytotoxicity were obtained [9]. Very recently, our group also investigated the controllable synthesis of lanthanide nano-phosphors through such inexpensive one-step approach [10,11].

Chemical sensing acts a crucial role in industry, analysis chemistry and biomedical fields [12,13]. It is accepted that chromium is widely used in the production of stainless steel, auto parts production, dyes preparation processes. As the valence of the chromium is increased, toxicity has been improved. Low valence element (Cr (III)) is an essential element of the human body and has the characteristics of promoting growth and development. Cr (VI) is rather harmful to organisms. A low concentration of infection may lead to kidney failure and rhinitis. Large quantity of infection can cause cancer [14,15]. Therefore, the detection of Cr (VI) in various environments has attracted much attention [16,17]. Over the past decades, Cr (VI) sensing has been mainly relied on advanced equipments including atomic absorption spectrum (AAS), ion chromatography (IC), inductively coupled plasma mass spectrum (ICP-MS) and X-ray fluorescence (XRF) [18]. These methods require sophisticate operation, longer measurement time and rigorous conditions. Thus, a green, sensitive and low-cost method for the recognition of Cr (VI) ion in real samples is highly expected.

Here, we report the efficient synthesis of carbon dots at room temperature by an ultrasonic vibrating one-step method based on m-phenylenediamine and citric acid as raw materials. Its quantum yield could reach as high as 65%. Dehydration, polymerization, carbonization, and passivation would occur during the ultrasound irradiations. The resultant nanoparticles possessed stable luminescence, uniform size and good water solubility. Moreover, the colorimetric and fluorescent properties of the carbon nanostructures were exploited and dual modes for the chromate detection could be realized based on carbon quantum dots. Very recently, a variety of precursors such as ethylenediamine, glucose or natural product (mushroom) were employed for the synthesis of carbon dots [19–23]. In this contribution, m-phenylenediamine and citric acid were also easily accessible and its resultant relative quantum yield could reach as high as 65%. In general, the most frequently-used strategy for the preparation of carbon dots was mainly based on a hydrothermal method and this process would extend from 4 to 10 h [19,21,22]. In order to accelerate the chemical reaction effectively, ultrasonic irradiation was used as the alternative candidate to generate massive energy to promote the carbonization process. Here the formation of carbon dots could be realized within 40 min. Compared with the previous reports [20], although the detection limit (0.14 μM) in this study was not the lowest, it still gave an acceptable value and this data was much less than the guideline limit proposed by World Health Organization (WHO) for Cr6+ concentration (0.96 μM). In addition, the determination of Cr2O7 2− was applied in real samples, which demonstrated that such nanoprobe was promising for the detection of Cr2O7 2− concentration in practical environment. The detailed structural, photophysical properties and sensing process were discussed in the following sections.

1,3-phenylenediamine, citric acid, and dimethyl sulfoxide were purchased from Alladin company. FeCl3, FeCl2, NaCl, CuCl2, MgCl2, AlCl3, KCl, CaCl2, ZnCl2, CuCl2, MnCl2, K2Cr2O7, KMnO4, NaNO2, NaF, NaBr, KI, NaS, Na2SO4, Na2S2O3, Na2S2O8, Na(SCN), Na2CO3, Na3PO4, H2O2 and N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Guangzhou Chemical Reagent Factory and used without further purification.

Fluorescence emission spectra were recorded by a computer-controlled F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer (HITACHI, Japan). Absolute quantum yields were explored at room temperature through a Hamamatsu Photonics absolute photoluminescence quantum yield measurement system. UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded with a UV-2700 spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). Lifetimes were measured by FLS920 Spectrometer (Edinburgh Instruments, UK) with a 370 nm irradiation as the excitation source. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were measured within the 4000–400 cm−1 region on an infrared spectrophotometer, Prestingge-21, using the KBr pellet technique. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were obtained on a Bruker D8/Advance X-ray diffractometer (Bruker, Germany). High resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) images were obtained with a JEM-1400PLUS TEM instrument (JEOL, Japan) accompanied by an operating acceleration voltage of 120 kV. Energy Dispersive Spectroscopy (EDS) and elemental mapping images were obtained by using a Phenom ProX. Dynamic light scattering (DLS) instrument (Malvern ZEN 3600, UK) was used for obtaining particle size distribution of the CDs. The fluorescence images were collected by using a camera of Canon EOS 6D. The pH values were determined with a digital pH meter PHS-3C (INESA, Shanghai, China) calibrated at room temperature (23 ± 2 °C) with standard buffers of pH 4.01, 6.86 and 9.18. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was performed by using an ESCALab220I-XL spectrometer (VG company, United Kingdom).

m-phenylenediamine (324.0 mg, 3 mmol) and citric acid (768.0 mg, 4 mmol) were dispersed in 10 ml N,N-Dimethylformamide (DMF) in a 15 ml flask. Then, the mixture was treated under ultrasound irradiation for 40 min and the solution was turned to faint yellow. The residual unreacted material was removed by using a cellulose ester dialysis membrane (MWCO: 500–1000) based on a 72 h dialysis procedure. For further purification, the dialyzed solution was rotary evaporated (75°, −0.1Kpa), to give a dark green viscous liquid. The resulting product was washed three times with ether. Then the product was freeze-dried for 48 h and stored in the ice box (−5°). For comparison, we also used o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine as raw materials to prepare carbon dots under the same conditions.

In the Cr (Ⅵ) ions sensing experiment, the CDs powder was dispersed in distilled water (abbreviated as DIW) at a concentration of 50 μg/ml. Different concentrations of Cr2O7 2− solution were added in the mixed solution. The mixture was stirred at room temperature for 4 min. The fluorescence spectra of the same volume solution were measured at a fixed excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Moreover, the sensitivity and selectivity experiments were also performed with other anions, metal ions and H2O2 under the same condition.
UV–vis absorption spectra were recorded in range of 200–800 nm in a similar manner to the fluorescent detection of Cr (Ⅵ) ions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of Cr2O7 2− in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: River water and drinking water were chosen for the analysis of real samples. Moreover, the river water was obtained from Zhujiang River in Guangzhou, China. The river water was filtered through a 0.30 μm microporous membrane and centrifuged at 15,000 rpm for 30 min to remove large particles. The drinking water was used for direct application. All the samples were adjusted to pH = 5. Since chromate (CrO4 2−) and dichromate (Cr2O7 2−) anions exist in a chemical equilibrium in aqueous solution (2 CrO4 2− +2 H+ = Cr2O7 2− + H2O), the movement of equilibrium was dependent on pH values. In this work, we tried to measure the concentration of Cr2O7 2− anions and the dichromate would be predominant in slightly acidic condition. Therefore, we controlled the pH value equal to 5.0. The procedure for the detection of Cr (VI) was the same as mentioned above.

Carbon dots (CDs) were assembled via an easy and one-step ultrasonic strategy based on 1,3-phenylenediamine and citric acid in DMF. The mixed solution was treated by 40 min ultrasound irradiation and a very stable CDs suspension of colloids was formed. Its synthetic route description was given in Fig. 1 . The prepared CDs were confirmed by microstructure studies as provided in Fig. 2 and Fig. S1. The resulting particles were in spherical shapes with excellent dispersibility as shown in Fig. 2a. High resolution images demonstrated good crystallinity of CDs with lattice edge distance of 2.05 Å, which was in consistent with diffraction plane of graphite (d (1,0,1) 2.03 Å) [24]. The lattice spacing and diffraction rings were also supported by selected area electron diffraction (SAED) patterns (Fig. 2b–d). The average size of the CDs was around 3–4 nm and the distribution file was presented in dynamic light scattering measurement (Fig. 2e).

The elemental composition of the carbon dots was explored via X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS) (Fig. S3). The survey of XPS spectrum gave rise to signals at 284.8, 399.8 and 531.8 eV, which were assigned to C1s, N1s and O1s respectively. The high-resolution C1s spectrum included the deconvoluted signals of C-C/C=C, C=N/C=O at 284.3 and 288.2 eV. In N1s spectrum, the characteristic signal was corresponding to pyridinic nitrogen at 398.3 eV. The high-resolution O1s spectrum was mainly composed of C=O and C-O groups at 531.6 and 533.1 eV respectively. FT-IR spectroscopy was capable of detecting the functional groups in CDs (Fig. 4b). The absorption band at 3450 cm−1was attributed to the stretching vibrations of -NH- bonds. The strong signal at 1718 cm−1 would be corresponding to the vibrations of carbonyl groups. The peak at 1523 cm−1was ascribed to the contribution of absorption band of N-H. The broad absorption bands at 1404 cm−1was related to the stretching vibrations of C-N. The peak at 1226 cm−1 was assigned to the vibrations of C-O bonds. The overall spectrum reflected the carbon dots might contain –CONH-linkages and amidation reaction would occur.

To study the optical properties of the CDs, UV–visible (UV–vis) absorption and photoluminescence (PL) spectra were investigated. UV–vis absorption curves were provided in Fig. 5 a (red line). Two intensive peaks at 260 nm and 300 nm were monitored and the absorption bands could be induced by π-π* transitions, which were derived from the conjugated structures. A broad band at around 350 nm was observed in the range of 320–400 nm and it was originated from the n-π* transitions of organic moieties (Fig. 5a Inset). The above peaks in the short (260 nm, 300 nm) or long wavelengths (350 nm) may be caused by the signals from carbon core and the surface respectively. Photoluminescence spectra in aqueous solution were presented in Fig. 5a. The emission peak was located at 440 nm upon the excitation at 370 nm. In contrast to the two precursors (citric acid and m-phenylenediamine, both weakly-emissive), the obtained carbon dots demonstrated strong blue luminescence and the emission might be related with the quantum effects, emission traps or excitons recombinations (Fig. 5b). We also explored the solvent effects on the carbon dots and the results supported the nanomaterial exhibited stable luminescence in the presence of water (Fig. 5c), indicating it could be utilized in pure aqueous environment. The isomers of phenylenediamines (o-phenylenediamine and p-phenylenediamine) were also employed to prepare the carbon dots under the similar conditions. But the achieved two nanomaterials exhibited much weaker emissions in the longer wavelengths (Fig. S4). We also measured the absolute quantum yields of the three nanomaterials and the carbon dots derived from o-phenylenediamine (19%) and p-phenylenediamine (8.5%) were much lower than m-phenylenediamine (65%). Therefore we did not develop their sensing functions. In addition, the emission feature was maintained its stability in the pH range between 2.0 and 13.0 (Fig. 5d). Accordingly, its excellent hydrophilicity and fluorescence stability under strong acid and alkali conditions will offer a wide range of possibilities for practical applications.

The time-resolved fluorescence decay curve measured by time-correlated double photon counting method was illustrated in Fig. 6 a. The blue emission was determined to be 14.78 ns. As mentioned in Fig. 6b, it was observed that when the excitation wavelength varied from 360 nm to 460 nm, the emission peak at about 440 nm continuously decreased and shifted to the longer wavelength at 528 nm. The results suggested a strong dependence on the excitation wavelengths. The origins of the emission changes would be caused by the existence of different emissive energy traps on the surface of carbon dots [25]. During this process, it was very attractive to achieve the result of single-excitation-induced-double emissions (excited at 390 nm) (Fig. S5). It might provide possibility for ratiometric fluorescence detection and several literature also reported the generation of dual emission properties and the exploration of their applications [26,27].

Any species to affect the radiative recombination of electron hole pairs from carbon nanoparticles would lead to sharp change in the photoluminescence. In this research, the interactions between CDs and Cr (VI) were studied. When various concentrations of Cr2O7 2− ion were added, the blue emissions were monitored (Fig. 7 a). As the concentration of Cr2O7 2− ion increased gradually (0–5 μM), its fluorescence intensity at 440 nm was reduced step by step. Fig. 7b gave rise to a typical linear correlation between the value of (F0-F)/F0 and the concentration of Cr2O7 2− (Y = 0.0002X +0.11, R2 = 0.97). Based on the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements, S is the slope of the calibration curve), the detection limit is calculated to be 140 nM. Compared with other sensor systems (Table S1), this material has excellent detection sensitivity. In order to test the selectivity of CD to Cr(VI), various anions, metal ions and oxidizer which have effects on the environment were measured under the same conditions, including Cr2O7 2−, MnO4−, NO2−, F−, Cl−, Br−, I−, S2−,SO4 2−,S2O3 2−, S2O8 2−, (SCN)-, CO3 2−, PO4 3−, Fe2+, Fe3+, Cu2+, K+, Ca2+, Na+, Mg2+, Mn2+,Zn2+, Al3+ and H2O2. The results displayed that the quenching effect of CDs on Cr2O7 2− ion was specific and highly selective to Cr2O7 2− ion (Fig. 8 ). Fig. 9 demonstrated the digital profiles of the carbon dots in the presence of diverse interferences under UV light irradiations and the solution showed unique response to Cr2O7 2− ion. As for the quenching mechanism, this “on-off” change would be due to the inner filtering effect of Cr2O7 2− ion [28]. As given in Fig. 10 , Cr2O7 2− ion had two broad absorption peaks at 360 nm and 440 nm. It was overlapped with the excitation (370 nm) and emission bands (440 nm) of the CDs. The released energy by carbon nanomaterial might be absorbed by Cr2O7 2− ion and the emission quenching was obtained [29]. The switched-off efficiency was greatly dependent on the absorption nature of Cr2O7 2−. The decay lifetimes of carbon dots in the absence and presence of Cr2O7 2− were measured and determined to be 14.78, 14.59 and 14.16 ns (Fig. S6). No substantial changes were observed and the results supported that energy transfer process could not be included. The main sensing mechanism would be based on the inner filter effect.

In order to facilitate the visualization of the targets through bare eyes, we have found that the solution turned from transparent to black in the presence of Cr2O7 2−. Therefore, UV–visible absorption spectra of CDs solutions at different Cr2O7 2− concentrations were explored. When the concentration of Cr2O7 2− increased (0–25 μM), the original absorption band at 260 nm was gradually decreased, and the absorption band around 350 nm was increased (Fig. 11 a). It was observed that there was a linear correlation between the value and the concentration of Cr2O7 2− (Y = 0.057X+1.069, R2 = 0.99). Based on the standard deviation rule (LOD = 3σ/s, where σ represents the standard deviation of 10 blank measurements, S is the slope of the calibration curve), the detection limit was achieved to be 410 nM (Fig. 11b). Further study of colorimetric experiment in different ions was carried out and the color change of Cr2O7 2− was visually recognizable compared to other species in the daylight (Fig. 12 ). The results proved that CDs had the selectivity for Cr2O7 2− ion in colorimetric detection. The color change of the solution might be ascribed by three factors. Firstly, the incorporation of Cr2O7 2− induced the gradual aggregation of tiny carbon dots and the expansion of particles would have influence on the color variation (Fig. S7). As for the absorption spectra, although the curve changes (decrease of peak at 261 nm and increase of peak at 300 nm) in the ultra-violet regions were not directly related to color change (Fig. S8), they would affect the absorption lines in visible light range. Finally, we could not rule out the influence of absorption band derived from Cr2O7 2− and it contributed to the band at 354 nm. But we have to say that the color of pure Cr2O7 2− solution was completely different from the carbon dots in the presence of Cr2O7 2− (Fig. S8, inset picture).

With the aim of investigating its application, this carbon nanomaterial was employed for the detection of Cr (VI) in the local Zhujiang River water and drinking water. As shown in Table 1 and Table 2 . Cr2O7 2− ion was measured in the Zhujiang River water, with recoveries ranging from 95% to 105%. In drinking water, the recoveries were ranged from 96% to 98%. These results further supported the feasibility of ion-selective recognition in practical environments. In terms of its convenience, fast responses and accurate detection, its utilization in new areas will generate considerable interests.

Carbon dots have attracted much attention due to their photoluminescence, biocompatibility and easy operation. In this study, we have developed a simple carbon nanomaterial for the rapid detection of Cr(Ⅵ) through a fluorescent “turn-off” effect and colorimetric recognition. Based on the effective synthesis via a one-step ultrasonic irradiation approach, the nanomaterial exhibited striking blue emissions and excitation-dependent properties were identified. A good linearity was obtained based on fluorescence measurement with detection limit down to 140 nM. The absorption spectra as well as photoluminescence also displayed enough selectivity and sensitivity compared with interfering species and detection limit was determined to be 410 nM. This research will provide a significant nanoplatform for sensing chromate with high accuracy and reliability.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Q. M. appreciates National Natural Science Foundation of China (No 21371063, 21328503) and Guangdong Science and Technology plan (2016A050502053). J. W. thanks supports from National Natural Science Foundation of China (No.51571094) and Innovation team project by department of Education of Guangdong Province (2016KCXTD009).

ce:section.ce:para:
The following is the supplementary data to this article:
revised supporting information revised supporting information

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2018.11.049.
